data.title: Maryland redistricting panel votes to recommend new congressional map
data.description:
Maryland’s Redistricting Advisory Commission voted Tuesday to recommend a new congressional map concept to the governor and state lawmakers, the panel announced. 

“At a moment when other states are moving aggressively to redraw maps, and when fundamental voting rights protections face renewed threats, Maryland has a responsibility to lead with urgency,” said Sen. Angela Alsobrooks (D-Md.), chair of the governor’s panel, in a release. 

“Our goal is to ensure our congressional delegation reflects the will of the people, protects representation for historically underrepresented communities, and gives Marylanders a Congress that can serve as a real check on this President.”

Alsobrooks noted that the vote is “not the end of public input,” encouraging Marylanders to review the proposed map concept and share feedback as it goes before the General Assembly, where lawmakers will need to introduce and pass a bill. 

Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) spearheaded the redistricting commission late last year to explore redrawing the state’s congressional maps, after GOP-led redistricting in Texas and a Democrat-friendly response in California ramped up the national redistricting fight across the country. 

Several other states have pursued mid-cycle redistricting ahead of the high-stakes midterms, as Republicans try to hold on to their narrow control of the House.

State lawmakers in nearby Virginia last week passed a proposed constitutional amendment that would allow lawmakers temporarily bypass the state’s redistricting commission to redraw a new House map by October 2030. 

But the Maryland effort faces an uphill climb, as Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson (D) spoke out in strong opposition last fall.

Ferguson, a member of the advisory commission, told WYPR last week that his objective “is to not jeopardize the current map, where we are in a good position to stand firm against the Trump administration.”

Democrats hold seven of the state’s eight House seats, but the new map could give them full control.

A poll conducted by the University of Maryland, Baltimore County last month found that roughly 4 in 10 Marylanders think the state’s district lines are currently drawn unfairly, compared to a little over a quarter who said they’re already fair.

data.title: Pressure over Greenland grows as Trump, allies descend on Davos
data.description:
President Trump will come face-to-face with European allies this week at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, as tensions build on both sides of the Atlantic over Trump’s insistence on the U.S. acquiring Greenland. 

On Saturday, the president slapped 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and its European allies to pressure them over the future of Greenland. Overnight, the president criticized the U.K. and shared a screenshot of a text conversation with French President Emmanuel Macron in which he voiced his discontent with Trump on the issue. 

The high-stakes meetings in Switzerland could play a significant role in determining the future of Washington’s involvement in the Danish territory.

U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer, who has led the country’s charm offensive on Trump during his second term, issued a rare criticism of Trump’s rhetoric on Greenland on Monday. 

“Any decision about the future status of Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland and the kingdom of Denmark alone,” Starmer said at a news conference. 

Meanwhile, Macron appeared to take a veiled jab at Trump during his address on stage at Davos, telling the audience, “conflict has been normalized.” The French leader said the “endless accumulation of new tariffs” is “fundamentally unacceptable” 

“Even more so when they are used as leverage against territorial sovereignty,” he added. 

Mick Mulvaney, who served as Trump’s chief of staff during his first administration, warned that Trump’s actions on Greenland are leading to a growingly isolated U.S. 

“In the first term, Trump was very clear to say ‘America First’ was not ‘America Alone.’ You get the impression that is changing in the second term,” Mulvaney said. 

Former U.S. Ambassador to Denmark Alan Leventhal, who served in the Biden administration, said the announced U.S. tariffs have only “unified” the European Union. 

“We should expect the EU countries to stand up to the threats of higher tariffs,” Leventhal said. 

In response to a question by The Hill during a briefing marking his first year in office, Trump said he still has a good relationship with Starmer and Macron, saying they get along “very well.” 

“They get a little bit rough when they’re, you know, when I’m not around, but when I’m around they treat me very nicely,” Trump said. 

However, when asked during the briefing whether he would attend the G7 meeting in Paris that Macron proposed to him over text, Trump said he would not. 

“No, I wouldn’t do that,” the president said. “Because you know, Emmanuel’s not going to be there for very long, and there’s not longevity there. He’s a friend of mine. He’s a nice guy.” 

Macron’s term as president of France ends in 2027, and he is term limited. 

Trump and his allies have repeatedly argued Washington controlling Greenland would bolster U.S. national security amid concerns Russia and China are growing their influence in the Arctic. 

“It matters to us because it matters to China and Russia,” said Nathan Sales, who served as the coordinator for counterterrorism and special envoy to the Global Coalition to Defeat ISIS during the first Trump administration. 

“China and Russia, long-term, will have an interest in exercising interest over Greenland, and that is not in the interest of the United States,” he added. 

Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov dubbed the debate over Greenland “a deep crisis” for NATO and its members. 

“It was hard to imagine before that such a thing could happen,” Lavrov said at a press conference Tuesday, arguing it could lead to an event in which “one NATO member is going to attack another NATO member.”

Mulvaney warned that a weakened NATO could be an unintended consequence of the deepening rift between Washington and its allies. 

“The Trump administration has got to figure out not only what the benefits are to acquiring Greenland but what the costs are to acquiring Greenland, and that’s more than just money,” he said. 

“If you ask me, ‘Is the NATO alliance at risk?’ Six months ago, I would have said no,” he continued. “Now, I think it’s different.” 

Trump predicted Tuesday that both sides of the Atlantic would be happy with an eventual agreement for the U.S. to take over the Danish territory. 

“I think we’re going to work something out where NATO’s going to be very happy, and we’re going to be very happy. But we need it for security purposes,” the president told reporters. 

Sales noted the diplomatic route as opposed to threatening other countries is one the Trump administration could take. 

“As important as Greenland is to the United States and our national security, we can get what we need more effectively by negotiating with our allies than by saber-rattling,” he said. 

“This is where the question of ends and means comes in,” Sales continued. “While we certainly have a strong interest in Greenland being firmly in the American camp, the means we use to bring that about matter, because Russia’s long goal going back to the Cold War has been to divide the transatlantic alliance.”

“They failed to do it during the Cold War, but they haven’t given up trying,” he added. 

Trump notably has not ruled out military force to acquire Greenland, but the White House has said diplomacy was always the first option. 

European nations have very noticeably taken note of the rhetoric. Troops from multiple countries arrived in Greenland last week, including from France, Sweden and Denmark. 

“The idea that the U.S. would attack the territory of one of our closest allies is beyond what anyone could have imagined before this administration,” Leventhal said. 

“Whatever comes out of President Trump’s visit to Davos, it does not change the massive consequences to our worldwide relationships if military action is taken in Greenland,” he continued. 

When asked at the briefing Tuesday how far he was willing to go to acquire Greenland, Trump responded “you’ll find out.”

data.title: Former NFL reporter Michele Tafoya files paperwork for Minnesota Senate race
data.description:
Former NFL journalist Michele Tafoya filed paperwork on Tuesday to run for Minnesota Senate, giving a boost to Republicans as they look to flip retiring Sen. Tina Smith’s (D-Minn.) seat.

Tafoya, who worked as a “Sunday Night Football” sideline reporter for NBC Sports Group until 2022, has been widely speculated to be a potential GOP Senate candidate. She has attacked Rep. Angie Craig (Minn.) and Minnesota Lt. Gov. Peggy Flanagan, both Senate Democratic candidates, on social media.

Tafoya’s entry into the race delivers Republicans a key recruit in the state and will force Democrats to spend at least some money in a blue-leaning state, especially as they have grappled with a statewide fraud scandal.

Tafoya’s decision to run is also likely to sharpen conversations among Democrats about electability. Craig has already pointed to her record of taking on tough Republican challengers in a competitive House seat compared with that of the more progressive Flanagan.

However, Republicans face some challenges of their own. For one, a Republican hasn’t won statewide in the North Star State since 2006, when former Gov. Tim Pawlenty won a second term.

And while the statewide fraud scandal has drawn increasing scrutiny around Gov. Tim Walz (D), who announced earlier this year he would be dropping his bid for a third term, the increasing federal immigration enforcement presence in Minnesota could also risk backlash against Republicans in the state.

data.title: Former NATO chief: Europe must prepare economic 'bazooka' to deter Trump's Greenland takeover
data.description:
Former NATO Secretary General Anders Fogh Rasmussen said Europe must ready an economic “bazooka” in response to President Trump’s threat to take Greenland by force.

“If Mr Trump does not accept Greenland’s democratic right to self-determination, Europe should first make clear the steps we are prepared to take—and this must extend beyond the threat of retaliatory tariffs,” Rasmussen said in a Tuesday piece for The Economist.

“Should the Trump administration attempt to change a sovereign European border, America must face the full force of the EU economic ‘bazooka’—with massive import and export restrictions and the cutting off of American companies from European public procurement,” he added.

Rasmussen was the prime minister of Denmark before serving as the head of NATO from 2009 to 2014. He noted that dozens of Danish soldiers died fighting alongside American troops in Iraq and Afghanistan, adding that he long viewed America as the leader of the free world.

“But in watching President Donald Trump’s hostile outbursts against one of America’s most loyal allies, I must now conclude that enough is enough,” he said.

Trump’s foreign policy moves throughout his second term have strained European relations, particularly his shift away from supporting Ukraine and skepticism toward NATO. Since returning to office, Trump has floated the idea of taking control of Greenland, which is a Danish territory, but he has ramped up his push to take it over in recent weeks.

Facing intense pushback from Europe and Canada, Trump has threatened additional tariffs on eight NATO allies. However, the president said Tuesday expressed optimism that the U.S. and European countries could find a mutually agreeable solution on Greenland.

“I think we’re going to work something out where NATO’s going to be very happy and we’re going to be very happy. But we need it for security purposes,” Trump told reporters.

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, whose own country has been targeted for acquisition by Trump, blasted Trump’s 10 percent tariffs targeting Denmark and other European countries.

“Canada strongly opposes tariffs over Greenland and calls for focused talks to achieve our shared objectives of security and prosperity in the Arctic,” Carney said at the World Economic Forum. 

During his speech in Davos, French President Emmanuel Macron accused Trump on Tuesday of wanting to “weaken and subordinate” Europe.

“It’s clear that we are reaching a time of instability, of imbalances, both from the security and defense point of view and from the economic point of view,” Macron said.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Comer rejects Clinton offer, teeing up contempt vote in aim to shift Epstein focus
data.description:
House Republicans on Wednesday will aim to shift heat over the Epstein files away from President Trump and onto Democrats as the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee votes on holding former President Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton in contempt of Congress over their refusal to appear for depositions after being subpoenaed.

That vote became a certainty on Tuesday, when House Oversight Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) said that he turned down an offer from the Clintons’ attorneys aiming for a compromise on the deposition dispute.

Comer said in a statement that the Clintons’ attorneys made an offer to have only former President Clinton testify before only Comer and ranking member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) in New York — with just two staff members each, no other committee members, and no official transcript.

Comer dismissed that proposal, saying it shows the Clintons “believe their last name entitles them to special treatment,” while taking a jab at the former president for being impeached in 1998: “Former President Clinton has a documented history of parsing language to evade questions, responded falsely under oath, and was impeached and suspended from the practice of law as a result.”

“Former Secretary Clinton’s on-the-record testimony is necessary for the Committee’s investigation given her knowledge from her time as Secretary of State of the federal government’s work to counter international sex-tracking rings, her personal knowledge of Ms. Maxwell, and her family’s relationship with Mr. Epstein,” Comer said.

Clinton spokesman Angel Ureña disputed that the former president’s team had requested there be no transcript.  

“We never said no to a transcript. Interviews are on the record and under oath. Whether it was written or typed isn’t why this is happening. If that were the last or only issue, we’d be in a different position. You keep misdirecting to protect you-know-who and God knows what,” Ureña wrote on X.

The Clintons’ attorneys did not immediately respond to a request for comment about the offer.

The panel had subpoenaed the Clintons along with a swath of other former government officials as part of its investigation into Jeffrey Epstein. But after months of negotiations between the GOP-led panel, the Clintons did not appear for scheduled depositions last week.

In letters and other statements, the Clintons insisted they provided the committee with information; provided sworn statements that they had no knowledge of criminal activities by Epstein or his associate Ghislaine Maxwell or about the government investigation into their sex trafficking; and argued that the subpoenas were legally invalid because they did not serve a legislative purpose.

While Republicans have released a number of the other former officials they subpoenaed from having to appear for a deposition, the high-profile nature of the Clintons’ personal friendship with Epstein and Maxwell has made them prime targets for Republican scrutiny.

Democrats, meanwhile, have argued that the focus on the Clintons is a calculated move from Republicans to distract from President Trump’s relationship to Epstein and from the Trump administration’s lack of compliance with a law he signed last year to release all the documents and information it has about the investigation into Epstein.

Yet Democrats also have an incentive to show they are pursuing investigations into Epstein impartially. Many key Democrats have not explicitly said that the Clinton subpoenas issued by the panel — which came after a unanimous voice vote following Democrats’ successful move to subpoena the Justice Department for the Epstein Files — are invalid.

“Look, we’ve said from day one that we’ll hear from anyone, whether they’re a Democrat or Republican, no matter their wealth or position of power,” Garcia said on CNN last week, before turning to criticism of the GOP leadership on the Oversight panel.

“While Comer continues partisan attacks on many people, particularly Democrats, he has done little to bring in the folks that know the most. What about Ghislaine Maxwell? Here is Jeffrey Epstein’s coconspirator,” Garcia said. “Why not bring her in to be deposed in front of the committee? What about [Attorney General] Pam Bondi, who’s released 1 percent of the files?”

Comer last week said he still plans to depose Maxwell, and that her lawyers previously said that she would plead the fifth in any interview until the Supreme Court ruled on her bid to appeal her conviction. And while he said Republicans “don’t like the speed” at which the Department of Justice is moving on producing documents, Comer argued the department is complying with releasing information.

Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), who along with Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) led the bill to force the Department of Justice to release the Epstein files, said on CNN last week that the Oversight panel should focus on the Clintons only after getting compliance from the Trump administration.

“Here’s a common sense way forward: Release all the files. President Clinton has called for releasing all the files. Once all the files are released, you can call President Clinton and Secretary Clinton or President Trump to come before our committee and testify,” Khanna said. 

“Comer is trying to distract with all of these things. Just get the files released, and then I do believe that the Clintons should come before the committee, but first release the files,” Khanna said.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) last week indicated he would bring the contempt measures to a floor vote if approved by the committee, but such a vote by the full House is not expected to occur until at least the first week of February, as the House has a scheduled recess next week.

data.title: Bus driver refuses to take passengers to DC destination, customer says
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) — A customer of the East Coast-based bus service BestBus, formerly known as DC2NY, says an overnight ride from New York City to Washington, D.C. turned into a nightmare — after the bus’s driver reportedly refused to take passengers to their destination.

The incident was “live skeeted” on social media site Bluesky by a passenger with the username “Musicology Duck.” The customer says they woke up from a nap to learn that “everyone on the bus has figured out we’re not going to the right place.”

Per Musicology Duck’s telling, after the bus switched drivers at a rest stop, the new driver reportedly “told everyone that they’re the ones on the wrong bus,” saying that the driver also refused to communicate with concerned passengers. Instead, according to MD, the driver just started driving “really fast.”

The speed and deviation from the route prompted many passengers to either call BestBus’ customer service or local police. MD says BestBus attempted to contact the driver but he wouldn’t answer the calls. Eventually, a customer got a BestBus representative on the phone and walked the phone to the driver, where the representative instructed the driver to go to Union Station, as scheduled.

At this point, the driver reportedly “screamed that he doesn’t get paid enough” and still refused to stop “erratically” driving toward Vienna, Virginia, where the driver eventually let passengers off, MD says.

BestBus sent another bus to pick up the passengers, both MD and the company itself say.

In a statement to Nexstar on Tuesday, BestBus said:

We are aware of an overnight incident involving one of our drivers on a recent trip. The experience described on social media does not reflect the standards we expect from our team or the service our passengers deserve.

We have launched an internal investigation and are reviewing all available information, including driver conduct, operational procedures, and trip conditions. While that review is underway, we have taken immediate steps to address customer impact. All affected passengers have received full refunds and a credit voucher for a future trip.

Musicology Duck, whom Nexstar attempted to contact, says that the driver “got everyone off the bus and drove off,” before police were able to arrive. The Bluesky user posted videos of the alleged driver, reportedly “wouldn’t give us his name,” MD says.

In its statement, BestBus said it is “committed to taking appropriate action and implementing any necessary improvements to prevent this from happening again.”

The thread went viral on Bluesky, with over 18,000 likes and over 4,000 reposts as of Tuesday afternoon. On Reddit, another user claiming to have been on the bus replied to a post about the incident in the Washington, D.C. subreddit that the Bluesky post was “wildly dramatic,” saying that “the guy had the wrong route and stubbornly refused to listen to either passengers or management.” Nevertheless, the user acknowledged that they understood why people were scared and said they wouldn’t book another BestBus trip unless they knew the driver was no longer working there.

Nexstar followed up with BestBus to ask about potential consequences for the driver but hasn’t heard back yet.

data.title: Katy Perry accompanies Trudeau in Davos
data.description:
Katy Perry was seen Tuesday alongside former Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Trudeau was spotted in a dark blue suit, while Perry was wearing a modest tan skirt set with a sweater. The two made their relationship official last October while the pop star was still on her “Lifetimes” tour which spanned across Budapest, Hungary; Kraków, Poland; Prague, Czech Republic, and Munich.

Trudeau, who resigned from office in March, separated from his ex-wife Sophie Gregoire in 2023, after 18 years of marriage. Perry broke off her long engagement to actor Orlando Bloom last year.

People, citing a source close to Perry, reported Sunday that the pop star “didn’t expect to fall for somebody so soon” after ending her engagement, but Trudeau “has a great sense of humor, he’s charming and treats her with respect.”

The lovers marked their territory in an official Instagram post last December with Perry showing herself and the former Canadian prime minister together in Japan. 

She captioned the post, “Tokyo times on tour and more,” and added a smiling face along with other emojis, including flowers and a red heart.

Trudeau also reposted a photo of the two on social platform X with Japan’s former prime minister, Fumio Kishida, and his wife, Yuko Kishida.

data.title: FTC plans to appeal Meta monopoly decision
data.description:
The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) said Tuesday that it plans to appeal a November ruling finding that Meta did not have a monopoly over personal social networking.

The agency, which sued the Facebook parent in 2020, suffered a key loss late last year when a federal judge determined the tech giant competes in a broader market with companies like TikTok and YouTube.

“Our position has not changed,” said Joe Simonson, FTC’s director of public affairs. “Meta violated our antitrust laws when it acquired Instagram and WhatsApp. Consequently, American consumers have suffered from Meta’s monopoly.”

“The staggering market power was on full display for everyone to see in 2020,” he continued. “Thankfully for Americans, there is an appeal process. We intend to file a notice of appeal to the U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit later today, and continue our fight for competitive markets and innovation.”

The FTC sued Meta in the first Trump administration for allegedly entrenching its social networking monopoly with its acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp.

However, U.S. District Judge James Boasberg underscored in his decision in November that the social media landscape has “changed markedly” since the agency first brought its case.

“Whether or not Meta enjoyed monopoly power in the past, though, the agency must show that it continues to hold such power now,” Boasberg wrote at the time. “The Court’s verdict today determines that the FTC has not done so.”

A senior FTC official argued Tuesday that the judge erred in his decision. The agency contends that TikTok and YouTube are not part of the same market as Meta’s products, which they suggest are instead focused on sharing and seeing content from family and friends.

The Hill has reached out to Meta for comment.

data.title: Netflix tops 325 million paid subscribers
data.description:
Netflix’s subscription business is booming.

The company said it had surpassed 325 million subscribers worldwide during a presentation to investors on Tuesday, increasing its operating income by 30 percent thanks to new customers added in the final quarter of 2025.

Revenues increased more than 15 percent in the last quarter of 2025, the company said, telling investors it plans to spend aggressively in the coming year on content creation and assets acquisition.

The most prominent of these goals is the company’s bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, the sprawling movie and television studio which Netflix is eyeing to buy with an all-cash bid totaling more than $70 billion.

The Netflix-Warner Bros. deal has raised concern among lawmakers and critics of media consolidation and will need to clear President Trump’s government regulators.

Media conglomerate Paramount has mounted a hostile bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, with top executives at the company lobbying Trump to support its push for the company over that of Netflix.

Netflix has made an aggressive push into pro sports, video podcasting and other live events as it seeks to set itself apart from fellow tech giants Amazon and Google, which have similarly poured hundreds of millions into their direct-to-consumer streaming businesses in recent years.

data.title: US forces apprehend 7th oil tanker in Caribbean
data.description:
The U.S. forces apprehended their seventh oil tanker in the Caribbean on Tuesday as the Trump administration continues to enforce its quarantine of sanctioned vessels in the region. 

The U.S. personnel, made up of officials from the U.S. Coast Guard, along with the U.S. Navy and Marines, seized the Motor Vessel Sagitta “without incident,” according to the U.S. Southern Command (Southcom), which shared a 20-second clip of the operation. 

“The apprehension of another tanker operating in defiance of President Trump’s established quarantine of sanctioned vessels in the Caribbean demonstrates our resolve to ensure that the only oil leaving Venezuela will be oil that is coordinated properly and lawfully,” Southcom said in the post. 

“These operations are backed by the full power our elite joint force team deployed in the Caribbean,” Southcom added. 

Last Thursday, the U.S. forces seized their sixth Venezuela-linked oil tanker, Veronica, which is part of the so-called ghost fleet, in the Caribbean. 

Earlier this month, Secretary of State Marco Rubio indicated that the U.S. would not govern Venezuela, after U.S. Special Forces captured Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, but said that the Trump administration would keep enforcing an existing “oil quarantine” on oil vessels coming in and out of the Latin American country.
